retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An indirect ophthalmoscope is also a handheld device but has a separate source of illumination from a headband and is almost universally performed by an ophthalmologist.  The indirect method provides a wider field of vision for the back of the eye than direct ophthalmoscopy. Pan ophthalmoscopy is a newer form of direct ophthalmoscopy that can be used without pupil dilation while providing a broad view of the fundus.  Slit-lamp ophthalmoscopy utilizes a fixed high-intensity light source and a handheld lens and is typically performed by an ophthalmologist.

retrieved_docs.title: Tests for Potential Vision -- Introduction -- Tests in Mildly Opaque Media
retrieved_docs.content: Contrast sensitivity - Helps detect defects in vision earlier than visual acuity. Photo stress test: This test helps in gross evaluation of retinal function, specifically the time taken for retinal pigment regeneration. Color vision - Evaluates the function of the cones in the macula. Certain types of color vision deficiency may indicate optic nerve disease. Amsler grid - Evaluates the integrity and shape of the macula. Two–point discrimination - Helps assess retinal function in uncooperative patients. Microperimetry - Helps in the mapping of scotomas. Fundus fluorescein angiography - Evaluates the retinal vasculature. Optical coherence tomography - Evaluates the shape and integrity of the macula and optic nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Slit-Lamp Biomicroscope -- Indications -- Indirect Retroillumination
retrieved_docs.content: In this method, the observer is at a right angle to the observed pathology or ocular structure. The pathology is seen against a dark, non-illuminated background. The pathologies can be divided based on the optical properties as obstructive, dispersive, or refractile. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Clinical Significance -- Multicolor Fundus Photography
retrieved_docs.content: The advantages of multicolor imaging include high contrast images, better resolution, reduction in image noise due to eye tracking mechanism, better comfort to the patient as laser beams are used for image capture instead of a bright flash of light, and the ability to image in a miotic pupil. The disadvantages include that it requires a slightly more extended fixation period and is highly operator dependent on acquiring artifact-free images. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Slit-Lamp Biomicroscope -- Indications -- Retroillumination
retrieved_docs.content: In this technique, the microscope is focused on the cornea, while the light is reflected from the iris or the retina. Retroillumination has been further classified as direct or indirect. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Asteroid Hyalosis -- Evaluation -- Ophthalmic Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: By ophthalmoscopy, ABs are small round refractile opacities associated with vitreous strands and suspended in the vitreous cavity. They move along with the movement of the vitreous body and get back to their previous position after the movement stops. (Supplementary video) However, they do not settle with gravity. The size of these particles is found to be 3 to 100 microns. [11] During ophthalmoscopy, the reflected light from these particles returns to the examiner and confounds the view. There occurs less reflection of light indirect ophthalmoscopy than in slit-lamp biomicroscopy with a 90 diopter lens or indirect ophthalmoscopy with a 20 diopter lens. So, direct ophthalmoscopy gives a relatively better view of the fundus in AH. The presence of AH in diabetic retinopathy eyes warrants fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA), especially in eyes with suspected neovascularisations. [17] With the advent of wide-angle FFA, not only neovascularisations but also peripheral lesions e.g., malignancies, can be detected. [18] Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) is being increasingly used as an alternative to FFA, and there are reports of its utility in detecting lesions in diabetic retinopathy. [19] Widefield fundus autofluorescence images are found to be helpful for the accurate localization of laser scars in the presence of AH. [20] Optical coherence tomography (OCT) helps in the study of the vitreoretinal interface in these eyes. [21] AH may induce media opacity during OCT scanning of the retina, and Swept source-OCT has superior diagnostic capabilities compared to SD-OCT in these settings. [22] B scan helps in the diagnosis of AH by the presence of moderate to high amplitude point spikes in the vitreous cavity separated by a clear zone from the retinal surface. [23] Recently, Reports of multicolor imaging of the fundus has been reported to be a preferred modality to detect retinal lesions that are difficult to be detected in color fundus photography. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Autorefractors -- Equipment -- Ocular Refraction Allowance for Visible Light Versus Near Infrared Rays
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the eye being achromatic, an allowance has to be calculated for the difference between visible light and NIR. This approximates to around 800 to 900 nm, for which the eye is 0.75 to 1 diopter sphere hypermetropic shift relative to 500 nm. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Clinical Significance -- Contrast-enhanced Angiography - Fundus Fluorescein Angiography and Indocyanine Green Angiography
retrieved_docs.content: Fundus fluorescein angiography is a technique of imaging the retinal vasculature by using sodium fluorescein dye. Indocyanine green angiography utilizes a higher wavelength that penetrates the retinal pigment epithelium and helps in imaging the choroidal vasculature. The transit of the dye through the retinal vasculature gives an idea of circulatory disturbances in the retina and choroid. When coupled with ultra-widefield imaging can give an idea of the dye transit through the entire retina and choroid. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Clinical Significance -- Ultrawidefield Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Ultra-widefield imaging enables to capture of the entire retina up to 200 degrees, depending on the commercial models used. This is especially useful in children and anxious patients who might be unable to fix in different gazes to image the different parts of the retina. The ability to capture such an extensive field in a non-mydriatic state is an added advantage and may be useful in patients in whom dilatation is contraindicated or unwilling for dilatation. It has a few disadvantages, like pseudocolor imaging and low posterior pole resolution. Though binocular indirect ophthalmoscope is the gold standard of examination of the retina, wide-field imaging may document and monitor progress in patients with vitreoretinal and choroidal pathologies. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Clinical Significance -- Multicolor Fundus Photography
retrieved_docs.content: Multicolor imaging of the fundus uses lasers of three different wavelengths to capture an image of the retina in contrast to the bright flash of light used to capture routine color fundus images. The blue (488 nm), green (515 nm), and infrared (820 nm) wavelengths depict information from different retinal depths. [34] The blue wavelength penetrates up to the inner retina and depicts information from the vitreoretinal interface - the retinal nerve fiber layer and the macular surface. The green wavelength penetrates up to the inner retinal layers highlighting details about retinal vasculature and the exudation of blood, lipids, and fluid in these layers. [35] The longer infrared wavelength penetrates up to the choroid and the outer retina and may depict changes in these layers. The field of view it provides is usually 30- or 55 degrees. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Fundus Camera -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Lipofuscin absorbs light in the blue spectrum with a wavelength of 470 nm and emits the light in the yellow-green spectrum at a wavelength of 600 to 610 nm. Lipofuscin-based autofluorescence is known as shortwave autofluorescence (SWAF) or blue auto-fluorescence (BAF). Melanin has an excitation wavelength of 787 nm and an emission wavelength at a near-infrared wavelength. Melanin-based autofluorescence is known as near-infrared autofluorescence (NIRAF). [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Exudative Retinal Detachment -- Evaluation -- Indocyanine green (ICG) angiography uses indocyanine green dye instead of fluorescein. Unlike fluorescein, indocyanine green fluoresces in the infrared or nonvisible light spectrum. The infrared wavelengths can penetrate the retinal layers, rendering the circulation in deeper layers visible when photographed with an infrared-sensitive camera.
retrieved_docs.content: In acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy, the early lesions typically exhibit hypofluorescence on fluorescein angiography. As the angiogram progresses into the late phases, these lesions become more defined in shape and size, often appearing larger than the placoid lesions seen clinically. As the lesions heal, the hypofluorescence in the late phases tends to become smaller and less distinct. [133] [134] [135] [136]

retrieved_docs.title: Indocyanine Green (ICG) Angiography -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: A fundus camera with excitation and a barrier filter is used for taking photographs. There are two categories of fundus cameras used for ICGA, digital flash cameras and confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscopes (SLOs). In a digital flash camera (including FF 450 plus by Zeiss and TRC-50DX by Topcon), the light source is white light. Its excitation filter transmits rays of wavelength between 640 and 780 nm and barrier filter between 820 and 900 nm. In SLOs (including Spectralis by Heidelberg, Mirante by Nidek, and California by Optos), laser monochromatic light is used for excitation. The Spectralis uses a diode laser (790 nm) for excitation. [33] A barrier filter of 830 nm is used in Spectralis. Dynamic ICGA is possible in the SLO system as it can capture 12 to 15 images per second. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the principle and mechanism of Scanning laser ophthalmoscope. Outline the various non-invasive and invasive dye base imaging techniques performed using a scanning laser ophthalmoscope. Summarize the indications and utility of Scanning laser ophthalmoscope in day-to-day practice. Review the clinical significance of scanning laser ophthalmoscope. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Fundus Camera -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscope (cSLO) based fundus cameras: The confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscope (cSLO) uses laser light instead of a flashlight to illuminate the retina. This reduces the scatter of light in the images acquired, forming a sharp, high-contrast image located within the focal plane.

retrieved_docs.title: Slit-Lamp Biomicroscope -- Indications -- Direct Focal Illumination
retrieved_docs.content: In this technique, the slit lamp beam is modified until it coincides with the correct focus of the microscope. The illumination is kept as a narrow slit at an oblique angle. The corneal and the lens tissue disperse light and become visible as bright objects against the dark background. The technique of direct focal illumination has three subcategories of examination. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy aids clinicians in recognizing the diverse presentations of choroidal and retinal pathology, facilitating accurate diagnosis, targeted management, and coordinated patient care across a range of ocular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: داء ريفسام, indicating that ocular examination of the retina can reveal manifestations of this disease and underscoring the importance of retinal assessment for accurate diagnosis and management of patients suspected of having داء ريفسام. Understanding the relationships among these insertion procedures, retinal‑targeted radiation therapies, and the ocular and renal sequelae of secondary diabetes mellitus enables clinicians to select appropriate interventional techniques, monitor disease‑specific complications, and provide integrated care that addresses both procedural interventions and systemic effects, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2055
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the eye are classified as a subcategory of histopathology procedures that are not placed in any other specific category, indicating that eye‑related diagnostic methods fall under the broader, uncategorized group of histopathological examinations.

retrieved_docs.content: and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive evaluation, multidisciplinary management and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1510
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid ophthalmologic and otologic preparations are a specific type of ophthalmological and otological preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2719
retrieved_docs.content: Glaucoma is classified as زَرَق, the Arabic term for “blue,” indicating that the condition is associated with a bluish discoloration of the eye.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1709
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق الشبكية (retinal tear) is directly linked to the need for repair of retinal tear; detachment, indicating that a retinal tear often requires surgical repair to prevent or treat detachment. This relationship highlights the importance of timely intervention for retinal tears to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus—all localized to the retina. The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation. Clinical assessment employs the concept of “general clinical state” to interpret both biologic function failure and uncontrolled disease conditions; uncontrolled disease conditions are associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the need for vigilant monitoring. Biologic function failure is linked to hepatic‑renal failure, indicating a critical organ‑system

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedure on anterior segment of eye is directly related to the eye (oči), indicating that surgical interventions targeting the front structures of the eye are procedures performed on the ocular organ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 935
retrieved_docs.content: The retina is the anatomical site where a range of ocular pathologies are observed, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and various forms of retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are all identified at the retina. Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has its findings localized to the retina. Recognizing that these diverse retinal findings share a common location highlights the importance of comprehensive retinal assessment in the management of diabetic eye disease and other retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the anatomical site for a range of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and various forms of retinopathy. Both mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified on the retina, indicating that diabetic retinal changes are localized to this tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has the retina as its finding site, further emphasizing the retina’s central role in ocular disease manifestation. Recognizing that these diverse abnormalities share the retina as their common location aids clinicians in targeted examination and management of retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1276
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a range of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various forms of retinopathy. In the setting of secondary diabetes mellitus, mild non‑proliferative retinopathy, non‑proliferative retinopathy, and retinopathy overall are all identified as having the retina as their finding site. Additionally, the retina is noted as the site of the condition described in Arabic as “داء ريفسام.” These connections emphasize the central role of the retina in the manifestation of diabetic retinal disease and other retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of thorough retinal evaluation in patient assessment and management.

Clinical assessment of disease severity, such as disease condition determination, uncontrolled, is interpreted by the general clinical state and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the importance of recognizing uncontrolled secondary diabetes mellitus in patient care.
